sentences.text: Yes.
I don't know, because when I went to the-- to the [INAUDIBLE] it was cold.
So it was in a rush.
I had to just in a few hours come to the-- to the company, my company.
So I didn't know what's happened to them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just-- just men in the army just went to the-- to the place when they collect the old soldiers.
They give uniforms and rifles and everything.
And--
Yes, everybody does military service, yes.
I think [INAUDIBLE].
21. 21 [POLISH] was it called.
They called us [POLISH] means children of Warsaw.
No.
He was not.
He was in a different company.
He was in the-- in the motorized company, when he ride the motorbike in the company.
Just a rifle.
And what's it called?
To dig-- to dig trenches-- a little--
It is a spade, yes.
It's a spade and a bayonet, of course.
They take me from Warsaw.
From the Warsaw citadel, we went with a very strong, fast march to Mlawa.
Mlawa was close-- close to the-- it's north, north of Warsaw, about 80 kilometers from Warsaw.
Oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and dozens.
Oh yes, yeah, of course, yes.
Oh, well-- well, after Mlawa, we have [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the blisters on the foot, and we're very tire.
And we start straightaway when we come.
We have to go to attack.
And the old fields was open.
The corn was in the-- the-- in the-- what would make the fields [POLISH], what they call in Polish.
It was burned.
They burned this, and we go to attack and-- and suddenly we weren't-- we hear on the left side and right side hurrah.
Hurrah, [POLISH].
And they-- they attacked the-- the trenches, the German trenches.
And-- and after a while, when they was shooting, it was quiet.
And the officers in the army, they keep all, and say, back, back, back, back, back, back-- to go back.
To-- to-- what means retreat, to go back?
Yeah.
Oh [INAUDIBLE].
They kill a lot.
But you never know, because every-- every-- every is thinking about his own life.
And you-- you don't look much at around.
You just-- when they said you have to run, you just run.
I-- no, I was not hurt, no.
[INAUDIBLE] run back.
It was very chaotic because nobody knows where to go and what to do.
And when you hear it in the night other soldiers tell you they-- they bombard their own-- own kitchen.
They bombarded their own kitchens.
They didn't know.
It was very chaotic.
And-- and when you run, you don't-- you don't look or anything.
You just start to-- to run [LAUGHS] because this is your life.
And you have to run.
So I-- I remember when I run, there was a few officers in the-- in the crowd.
I think there was a produce wagon.
And I jump on the back-- on the back bumper bar.
I jump on the back bumper, and I couldn't-- I haven't got to-- to keep my hands to-- to say.
So I turned to [?
Burnett, ?] and [LAUGHS] I make a hole in the roof.
And I keep my hands-- they didn't know what happened.
They was afraid somebody wants to kill us.
Yes, it was just all right.
They take you to-- to back.
I remember the-- the name of the-- of the of the cities or the-- the villages.
I don't remember the names.
I went back to Warsaw, yes.
I went back to Warsaw after two or three years.
And the most was they make the-- they want to make them orderly, collect the soldiers to put them on the-- on the-- on the-- on the highway.
Right It was huge from both sides.
And they keep them [INAUDIBLE] and the-- the German Luftwaffe bombard it, and makes a mess, really.
Not most, but many.
I don't know because you never know.
Of course, there was many killed, but you see killed soldiers, but you don't know how many.
But you see killed soldiers, horses, and everything mixed up the [INAUDIBLE].
We got back to Warsaw, they start, again, organize the soldiers to keep some [POLISH], as the call to protect Warsaw from the Nazis.
And I was in the Warsaw squadron.
It was close to what they call [POLISH] applies.
And we-- we-- we sit there and-- with a few rifles.
And we want to-- to-- to stops them.
But you know, the houses in-- in-- in-- in-- in [INAUDIBLE] but the houses are not compact together, but different houses.
And they live under the houses to-- to have some protection and shoot them.
And-- and-- in a few nights, we attacked, but they-- they-- they-- they have prepared items, have prepared the [NON-ENGLISH] what they call the special weapons.
But they shoot, and they-- and they-- and they-- and they shoot out, and the shrapnel, they fell after the houses.
It was-- yes, but, so they have to give up, and-- and they take us to-- to leave-- the-- the rifles, to leave everything.
And they put in different places, and leave everything and go home.
When I got home, I say we were going to run from the place.
When I come home. it was destroyed, was burnt, and-- and-- but everybody survived.
And they went to my brother's place.
I make a decision to-- to-- to go away, to run away, because to stay in Warsaw means to go every day to-- to-- to clean the streets.
They take from the-- from the ghetto.
It was not a ghetto at this time when I run.
But they collect the Jews, and-- and they-- they have to clean the-- the-- the city.
And yes, my wife has an uncle in Brzesc.
It's-- it's the other side of the the-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- means the border between-- between-- between German and Russia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It was through-- through the borders [INAUDIBLE].
And when-- when we-- [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We find a peasant with a-- with a carriage and a horse, and he take us to the border.
And we go through the border.
On the border, yes, there was a German-- German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
A German guard, yes.
And they-- they asked me where we're going.
We are from Brzesc.
And we're going to Brzesc.
And they search me [INAUDIBLE] and my wife.
They search my wife.
And we went.
And they took everything that we have.
They say-- my wife is Jewish, they say.
But you are not Jewish.
You stay up here to me.
I don't know.
They didn't recognize me as Jewish.
Yeah.
Yes.
We have-- we have-- we have money that they take from us.
And I have-- and I have a bottle of vodka that they take from me and some jewelry [INAUDIBLE] my wife they took.
No, no, they didn't take those.
Clothes?
No, no.
No, no.
When we went to her uncle, he-- he runs a shop in Brest.
And-- and this time it was-- it was-- the-- it was how to say-- a pact-- a pact there, an understanding between the Hitler and-- and Stalin, So they take Poland to [POLISH].
[POLISH] means the river, the border.
And the Russians take the parts, and German the this part.
Yes, yes.
Yes.
And my-- her uncle decide to go away to-- he liquidate his shop.
And he decide to go away.
And he-- he told us he was going.
He find some people who can smuggle him to Japan and through Japan to the United States.
Yeah.
Yes, he [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, but then we didn't go.
My wife was pregnant and-- and everything.
We think it would be better if we stay.
But then we stay and start the-- the war with Russia-- Germany with Russia start the war.
So we have to go somewhere.
We went to Pinsk.
It's a small city, many Jews.
And from Pinsk, we went to-- I was working there a while, and from Pinsk, we went to Janow Podlaski.
Where is this?
It's in between Pinsk and-- and-- and-- and [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes, it was Soviet Union, because-- yes.
Pardon me?
Is it Belarus?
Yes, Belarus, yes.
No, I couldn't speak the Russian at this time.
But I learned fast, yes, later on, yes.
Yes, oh yes, yes.
I find work, yes.
[INAUDIBLE] yes, I find, yes.
I was-- in Pinsk, I was working.
It was a-- a-- [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a nursery, a [INAUDIBLE] nursery, like a-- like a [INAUDIBLE]-- like a [INAUDIBLE] nursery.
And it was a Pole yelling.
His name-- and he run with the German.
So it was a post up here.
And I think over the nursery in Pinsk.
And from Pinsk, I went to-- to Janow Podlaski.
In Janow Podlaski, I work for a Russian-- Russian institution.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH] means a-- a special [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] company who runs-- who runs farms and, and the all produce-- distributed all produce for the-- for the-- for the company.
It was state.
I don't think that a cooperative is state.
It's run by the state, not cooperative.
And yes, I was in a-- a good position, because I have to travel a lot because we haven't got seeds.
And I have to travel a lot to Ukraine direction to collect some seeds from the farmers, to buy seeds, because I haven't got seeds.
And and I was carrying a lot of money with me, and I was buying seeds and sending to-- to-- to-- to-- Janow Podlaski.
And-- and-- and where I have the possibility to go a lot, I-- I-- I visit my brother in Lvov.
That was Andrew.
It was Andrew.
I visit him in Lvov, and we've been a few hours or dine together.
And I told him what I am doing, and-- and I then went back.
No, he didn't know.
He just left them and his place and went away, yes, yes.
Absolutely not, no news, no.
Oh, we know about the ghetto, but we didn't know what's happened, and who is in the ghetto.
No, absolutely not.
Yes, we sent letters.
We sent letters, but I never receive a letter.
No.
And was your wife working too?
Yes, she was working too.
Yes, she was always working, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
The baby was born in in-- in '39.
No, '39 was the war-- yes, in '40.
Yes.
Yes, I was working in a-- in a farm around the time that the German come.
Yes, this was before they destroyed the ghetto.
Yes, I used to work still in the German when they come in.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, they collect-- I didn't see them because I was in the field, but later on when I come in the-- in the-- in the-- into town, they told me it was a group of German SS who it was.
And they-- they took the-- the men-- only man.
They took over 100 men And between all the men was my-- my-- my wife's father.
And he-- he-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and yes, and they took them.
They took them, and they-- and they rode.
And-- and they shoot them.
Later on, I saw the-- the peasant with carriages.
They-- they-- they carried the bodies in the carriages somewhere.
I don't know where they took them.
Just went the come.
Just-- it was-- '42, I think it was. '41, '42, yes, when they start organize the ghetto, and they surround the ghetto with-- with wire.
And-- and-- and-- and this time, I was in field, and I come back.
On the road when I go back, some-- some citizen sitting and told me, don't go there, because the ghetto is surrounded.
And they-- they-- they-- they shoot everybody.
Don't go there.
Oh, in the ghetto a few months.
I don't remember exactly how long.
Yes, I take to work, yes.
No.
But this was-- was-- the Germans didn't take too to do work.
Everybody was working somewhere for the Russians.
And still, the whole-- the whole structure of the-- of the work was the same.
Again, everybody was going on before to work.
And-- but-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes.
And when I come back, and I meet some-- a Jew with a boy.
And we go together to the ghetto.
And the ghetto was burned.
It was on fire.
And we stay in a shed opposite-- opposite the street in a shed.
Here was a local person.
He know exactly everything, because he was born there.
He lived his whole life in Janow Podlaski in the-- in the little town.
So he knows everything.
And He told me, come with me.
And I went with him.
And we went in the shed.
And we just stayed in the shed until late night.
And we saw the ghetto burning.
And we saw the-- the-- the machine guns around.
And the shot-- we heard shots and burning the ghetto, and shots [INAUDIBLE] because the people are running from the fire.
And they shot everybody.
Yeah.
And later on, he say, come with me I know-- I know here a person, because he made a business with some local people, and then-- and then-- called Hala.
Come with me.
He'll give us a place to stay overnight and tomorrow [INAUDIBLE] what to do.
So we went-- I went with him.
And he opened the shed and put us in the shed.
And in the straw, we lie in the straw, and we lie through the night.
And in the-- in the early morning, we hear, like, soldiers are walking on the-- on the-- on the stones, because everything was stones in the-- the yard.
And we hear the soldiers are coming.
And he opened the-- the-- the gate-- the gate.
And they are staying with the-- with the rifles and the bayonets.
[GERMAN].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they took us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
In the column, we were walking in a column, and this time the-- the people who run away, they find.
They collect, and they take them in the marketplace, and they shoot in the marketplace.
And when we are walking-- I don't know how many, maybe 20 people or less are walking-- somebody, a soldier, or a policeman are coming, and say, halt.
Don't take them to the marketplace.
They're finished there.
This place is finished.
Take them to the-- to the-- to the-- to the arrest-- to the-- to the jail.
It was a little jail-- two-- two-- two rooms and a corridor-- a little jail.
And they put us in the jail.
They put us in the jail.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was sitting in the corridor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I look up.
And I aw-- and I saw the loft.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I think I'll jump in the loft.
And I went, people, help me.
Help me to go in.
And I went in the loft.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was sitting here, and I think how people are, oddly enough, honest not to say to the German somebody run.
But nobody did say anything.
Sorry.
No, a boy-- a boy come.
A boy come over.
[DRINKS] Not a boy-- a young man.
I don't know him.
I didn't know him.
[INAUDIBLE].
And he come, and we sit, both of us.
And nobody else want to-- to-- to jump in.
So we-- we look around, what to do, how to run from here.
[CRYING] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] I think it is a little [INAUDIBLE] like a window from one side, from the backyard And I think this could be the way we can run.
But it was very high.
So I think the-- the vice.
We took the vice, and with the vice we can do something to jump.
And it was dark.
And we see through the-- through the planks in the wall.
We see when they take everybody from the jail outside in-- in the garden.
And-- and they take-- take-- they tell them to take their clothes off.
They take-- and then they take the clothes.
We are sitting and looking at everything what's happening there.
And they take the clothes.
And they took them away without the clothes, just to-- to prepare before they have the trenches with-- with-- with-- with-- [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes, ready to bury.
And it was quiet.
Nobody was there.
And so I think we wouldn't jump through the window.
And I have a look through the-- through the-- through the little door.
Look down, and nobody's there.
And I said, I jump when somebody's standing, I jump on him from the back, and I-- maybe I can take the rifle from him.
Oh, but when I jump down, and I say to him nobody's here.
Nobody's here.
And I said, come on.
Come down.
And we go down, and I tell him not to run.
And we walk.
We walk back in the backyard.
And we go through the back yard to the main road-- to the main road.
Nobody saw us.
One saw us because the [INAUDIBLE] place where they collect the milk from the-- from the-- they call this a milk collection place, where they collect milk from-- from the-- from the peasants.
And there was somebody standing.
He saw us.
He saw us.
But he didn't know he was looking.
He maybe didn't know it was Jews running.
But just we go.
We went.
